followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I raise the overall score modestly from 5.6 to 5.9 because the other reviews made a good case that the epsilon-form mechanism and worked examples are coherent and novel. I also downgrade my cut-tubing concern from major to moderate because it may be an edge-label convention obscured by the HTML rendering, but I keep it as a real human spotcheck.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: I do not treat the logarithmicity of the forms in equations (27)-(28) as a separate major missing assumption; they are written as wedges of dlogs, modulo generic nonvanishing of arguments.

I do not find the sign/projective-invariance objection around equation (16) well supported from this bundle, especially because the later construction uses the reduced logarithmic forms rather than relying solely on that projective form.

I do not think uniqueness of the decomposition in equation (43) is required for the existence of some differential system; nonminimal or overcomplete spanning sets can still define a chosen connection, though minimality would remain unclear.
I disagree with the more optimistic reading that the arbitrary-graph proof gap is only moderate for a mathematics-paper review; if the general statement is claimed, the missing proof is central.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The boundary-term objection is real: the integration-by-parts step needs explicit twisted-cycle or convergence hypotheses.
The load-bearing conjecture that the coefficient in equations (47)-(49) is always a region variable is the main technical gap.
The paper deserves credit for a clear positive-geometry alternative to kinematic-flow bases, even though it uses more master functions.
The growth in the number of tubing-labeled functions is a practical caveat, though I treat it as utility rather than correctness.

main_claims.claim: FRW wavefunction coefficients can be represented as twisted integrals of logarithmic forms built from flat-space wavefunction and amplitube data.
main_claims.notes: The construction is well motivated by the cited amplitube relation, but the paper itself lists the general proof of equation (26) as future work.

main_claims.claim: For logarithmic differential forms, applying the kinematic differential to the twisted integral gives a canonical epsilon-form system proportional to twist parameters.
main_claims.notes: The dlog algebra is persuasive, and the other reviews strengthened my view that this is the cleanest technical idea in the paper. The support is still conditional on unstated integration-by-parts and contour hypotheses.

main_claims.claim: The functions labeled by cut tubings form a closed system of differential equations whose coefficients are dlogs of region variables.
main_claims.notes: The examples support the pattern, but the text explicitly says the key coefficient being a region variable is conjectural for general graphs.

main_claims.claim: The coefficient matrices admit a tubing-labeled diagonalization with eigenvectors and eigenvalues given by equations (55) and (56).
main_claims.notes: This is clearly presented as checked in examples and conjectured in general, so it is an interesting observation rather than an established theorem.

main_claims.claim: The approach works uniformly for arbitrary graphs, including graphs with cycles.
main_claims.notes: The paper provides small examples, including a two-vertex cyclic multigraph, but the general combinatorial proof and some edge-deletion conventions remain to be checked.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A precise class of twisted cycles or integration contours for which total derivatives integrate to zero.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without this, equations (40)-(42) may acquire boundary contributions.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Genericity conditions on kinematic variables, region variables, and twist exponents.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The dlog system assumes nonzero logarithmic arguments and avoids singular or resonant degenerations.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A graph-theoretic lemma guaranteeing that the reduction in equations (47)-(49) always yields region-variable coefficients.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is the local combinatorial step needed to turn the examples into the all-graph equation (53).

missing_assumptions.assumption: A general proof of the cut-tubing expansion of the flat-space form, including orientation and sign conventions.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The target wavefunction coefficient is connected to the proposed master integrals through this decomposition.

missing_assumptions.assumption: An edge-decorated convention for cut components in graphs with cycles, multiple edges, and possible self-loops.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The region variables and tubing labels must retain enough edge data after cuts for cyclic and multigraph examples.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the original TeX/PDF define cut tubings with enough edge data to distinguish non-bridge cuts in cyclic multigraphs?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The HTML rendering suppresses notation, and the intended convention may be encoded graphically or in TeX labels not visible in the bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does equation (53) hold for small cyclic graphs beyond the displayed two-vertex multigraph, such as a triangle or square?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires explicit symbolic enumeration of cut tubings, region variables, and dlog reductions beyond the examples shown.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the integration-by-parts manipulations valid for the physical FRW contours and twist parameters, possibly after analytic continuation?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper does not specify the analytic cycle, branch choices, or convergence domain needed to audit the boundary behavior.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the claimed 18-dimensional basis and block structure for the three-vertex path graph correct?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The bundle does not provide the full list of tubings and all matrix entries, so this needs direct combinatorial verification.

novelty_assessment: The other reviewers fairly emphasize that the paper has a genuinely clean angle: staying inside logarithmic forms gives a direct route to canonical-form differential equations, and labeling the basis and coefficients by the same tubing combinatorics is elegant. I agree this is more than routine repackaging of kinematic flow. However, the novelty is presently attached to a conjectural framework with examples, not to a completed general theorem.
score_rationale: The paper has a strong and elegant organizing idea, and the examples make the proposal credible. The score remains below the strong/promising range because the central all-graph closure, the cut-tubing decomposition, and the diagonalization are not proved, while the analytic hypotheses for the twisted integrals are not stated. This is a worthwhile conjectural contribution, but still contested as a mathematics paper.
summary: The paper proposes a positive-geometry and graph-tubing construction of canonical differential equations for FRW wavefunction coefficients. After reading the other reviews, I am slightly more positive about the novelty and clarity of the worked examples, but my main judgment is unchanged: this is a promising conjectural framework, not a theorem-level derivation for arbitrary graphs. I do not claim to certify the proof; the central all-graph closure and eigensystem remain unproved, and the analytic setup for the twisted integrals is under-specified.

technical_objections.evidence: Section 3.3 says the needed elimination is found case by case and conjectures that the coefficient is always a region variable. The conclusion lists formula (53) among results whose general proof remains open.
technical_objections.objection: The all-graph closure formula is not proved, even though it is central to the claimed differential equations.

technical_objections.evidence: Equation (26) is presented as a rewriting feeding directly into the master-integral basis, but the conclusion explicitly includes formula (26) among statements still lacking a general proof.
technical_objections.objection: The decomposition of the flat-space form into all cut-tubing forms is used before being established generally.

technical_objections.evidence: The derivation says boundary terms are dropped. For twisted integrals this depends on the twisted cycle and on the real parts or analytic continuation of the twist exponents; these hypotheses are not stated.
technical_objections.objection: The integration-by-parts step drops boundary terms without specifying contours, convergence, branch choices, or analytic continuation.

technical_objections.evidence: Unary tubes are defined as vertex-induced connected subgraphs, while cut tubings are built by deleting subsets of edges. Deleting a non-bridge edge can leave the same vertex set with different remaining edge data, so the notation must record edge-decorated component data to distinguish cases such as the two-edge cycle.
technical_objections.objection: The definition of cut tubings is not fully precise for non-bridge edge deletions in cyclic graphs and multigraphs.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states that the eigensystem has been checked for many graphs and leaves a general proof for future work.
technical_objections.objection: The diagonalization formula is conjectural rather than a proved structural result.
